skill.name: agent-memory-mcp
skill.description: A hybrid memory system that provides persistent, searchable knowledge management for AI agents (Architecture, Patterns, Decisions).
skill.summary: Persistent memory system for AI agents with searchable knowledge management and MCP server integration.
skill.author: Amit Rathiesh

security_audit.summary: All static findings are false positives. The skill contains only documentation with setup instructions (git clone, npm commands). The flagged 'external_commands' are legitimate bash commands in documentation, not executable code. The flagged 'network' URLs are documentation references (GitHub repo URL, localhost dashboard). The flagged 'cryptographic' terms ('key', 'risk') are common English words used in their normal sense, not cryptographic algorithms.

content.user_title: Enable Persistent Memory for AI Agents
content.value_statement: This skill provides AI agents with persistent, searchable memory to retain architecture decisions, patterns, and knowledge across sessions without manual tracking.
actual_capabilities: Search memories by query, type, or tags using memory_search tool
Write and store new knowledge or decisions with memory_write tool
Retrieve specific memory content by key using memory_read tool
View analytics on memory usage with memory_stats tool
Run a local dashboard to visualize memory data
limitations: Must clone external agentMemory repository for full functionality
Memory is local to the MCP server instance

use_cases.title: Retain Architecture Decisions
use_cases.description: Store and retrieve architectural choices made during project setup so future sessions understand why certain patterns were selected.

use_cases.title: Build Pattern Library
use_cases.description: Collect and search reusable code patterns, snippets, and best practices discovered during development.

use_cases.title: Track Project Context
use_cases.description: Maintain persistent context about project goals, constraints, and conventions across agent sessions.

prompt_templates.title: Save Architecture Decision
prompt_templates.prompt: Use memory_write to store this architecture decision: key: '{decision_key}', type: 'decision', content: '{decision_content}', tags: ['{tag1}', '{tag2}']
prompt_templates.scenario: When you make an important architectural choice during development

prompt_templates.title: Find Authentication Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Search the memory for authentication patterns using memory_search with query: 'authentication', type: 'pattern'
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to recall previously documented authentication approaches

prompt_templates.title: Recall Design Discussion
prompt_templates.prompt: Retrieve the design discussion about '{topic}' using memory_read with key: '{memory_key}'
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to reference past design discussions

prompt_templates.title: Review Memory Statistics
prompt_templates.prompt: Show memory statistics using memory_stats to understand what knowledge has been stored
prompt_templates.scenario: When you want to see the memory usage and stored knowledge overview

output_examples.input: Search for all stored patterns related to authentication
output_examples.output:
Found 3 memories:
- auth-oauth2: OAuth2 implementation pattern
- auth-jwt: JWT token handling pattern
- auth-sessions: Session management pattern

output_examples.input: Write a new architecture decision about database choice
output_examples.output:
Memory saved successfully:
Key: db-choice-postgres
Type: decision
Content: Selected PostgreSQL for its relational integrity and JSON support
Tags: database, architecture, production

Memory Stats:
- Total memories: 24
- Types: decisions(8), patterns(12), knowledge(4)
- Top tags: authentication(5), database(4), api(3)

best_practices: Use consistent naming conventions for memory keys to make retrieval predictable
Add relevant tags when writing memories to improve searchability
Periodically review memory_stats to ensure the knowledge base remains organized
anti_patterns: Storing sensitive information like API keys or passwords in memory
Creating duplicate memories with slightly different keys instead of updating existing ones
Using overly generic search queries that return too many unrelated results

faq.question: How does this skill persist memory across sessions?
faq.answer: The skill runs as an MCP server that maintains a local storage file. As long as the server runs, memories persist. Restarting the server with the same project_id loads existing memories.

faq.question: Can I share memories between different projects?
faq.answer: Memories are stored per project_id. You would need to manually export/import memory data to share across projects.

faq.question: What happens if I forget my memory key?
faq.answer: You can use memory_search to find memories by content, type, or tags even if you do not remember the exact key.

faq.question: Is the dashboard accessible remotely?
faq.answer: No, the dashboard runs on localhost only for security reasons. It is not exposed to external networks.

faq.question: Can I delete or update existing memories?
faq.answer: The current version supports writing new memories. For updates, you would write a new memory with an updated key or content.

faq.question: What types of content can I store?
faq.answer: You can store any text content including architecture decisions, code patterns, meeting notes, API specifications, and project conventions.
